card maintained by the property/supply officer as against the equipment ledger
cards maintained by the accounting and the total thereof shall be reconciled with
the control accounts in the general ledger;
• the General Services Officer to update the Property Cards in order to provide the
balances per card in the RPCPPE and immediately identified the missing properties
or discrepancies, if any, of the PPEs on record against the actual count. Likewise,
Acknowledgement Receipt of Equipment (ARE) for PPE’s acquired on prior years
should be updated. Ensure that the Acknowledgement Receipt of Equipment was
renewed at least every three years or whenever there is a change in custodianship
to establish proper accountability;
• the Municipal Accountant to prepare the lapsing schedule with complete
information consistent with the balances presented in the financial statements;
• the Municipal Accountant to trace documents of all PPEs and provide the
corresponding depreciation expenses; and
• the Municipal Accountant to update the required Property Plant and Equipment
Ledger Card to facilitate the reconciliation of RPCPPE with the accounting records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
adhere strictly to the provisions of the aforesaid PPSAS and properly identify/classify
the current and non-current portion of long-term loans payable in the Statement of
Financial Position. The Municipal Account must draw the necessary journal entries to
reflect the proper classification of the amount.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
• the Municipal Budget Officer certify into the existence of available appropriation as evidenced by preparation of Obligation Request for all disbursements including cash advances that were granted by the Municipality except disbursements in the Trust Fund.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant check the attached supporting documents before signing the disbursement voucher as to its obligated allotment and completeness of the supporting documents.

We recommended that the purchase goods/service for the conduct of various activities of Municipality should be properly planned and made through the prescribed modes of procurement under RA 9184, instead of through cash advance. In all instances, the procuring entity shall ensure that the most advantageous price for the Municipality is obtained.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer refrain from granting cash advances to accountable officers which payments can be made directly to the supplier/contractor in order for the Municipality to deduct and withhold applicable taxes and remit the same in accordance with the BIR Rules and Regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
- the Municipal Accountant to exercise utmost diligence in monitoring liquidation of cash advances by properly ensuring that cash advances are liquidated as soon as the purpose for which it was given has been served. If the purpose was not served for the grant of cash advance, require the immediate refund of the amount of cash advance;
- the Disbursing Officer to enforce a cut off in disbursing money out of the cash advance for a timely liquidation and make a refund, if necessary;
- the Municipal Accountant to immediately issue demand letter enjoining the concerned official with outstanding cash advance to liquidate the amount. Cause the withholding of salary of the Accountable Officers (AOs) who failed to settle/liquidate their cash advances despite the issuance of demand letters in accordance with Section 5.9 of COA Circular No. 97-002; and
- the Municipal Accountant to acknowledge the liquidation documents received from various employees by signing the Liquidation Reports and when such reports were received for proper monitoring.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
• the Municipal Accountant prepare the journal entry for the transfer of the
unexpended LDRRMF balance of the 70% Mitigation Fund- MOOE and the 30%
QRF as of year-end to Trust Liability-DRRMF account;
• the Municipal Budget Officer verify the existence of the appropriations for a
certain obligated project before issuing/signing the Obligation Request;
• the Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer effect
the transfer of funds amounting to ₱1,045,000.00 to the General Fund; and
• the MDRRMO designate ensure that the submitted Report on the Sources and
Utilization of DRRMF is compliant with the COA Circular 2012-002 and that the
amounts reflected in the report is fair so as not to mislead the users of the report.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
• the MENRO to implement strictly the provisions of RA 9003 and intensify the “No
Segregation, No Collection Policy” among residents of every barangay; and
• the Solid Waste Management Board to monitor and encourage compliance by its
component barangays by strictly enforcing the operation of the Materials Recovery
Facility particularly those barangays noted with inactive MRF.

We also recommended the Municipal Officials and Employees to exercise strong
political will to implement the environmental laws on preserving and protecting the
environment as mandated by the Act.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
• the Municipal Engineer to require the contractor complete the project as soon as
possible and impose applicable sanctions and penalties, if warranted in
accordance with Sections 8 of Annex E (Contract Implementation Guidelines for the
Procurement of Infrastructure Projects) of the Revised Implementing Rules and
Regulations (IRR) of RA 9184; and
• the Municipal Engineer to compute the amount of liquidated damages to be
deducted on the payments to be made to the contractor.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
• Bids and Awards Committee to observe the provisions of the IRR of R.A. 9184 in
the posting of Notice of Award and Notice to Proceed together with the approved
contract in the agency website and PhilGEPS and require the BAC Secretariat to
issue certification on the posting of aforementioned documents in any
conspicuous places in the premises of the procuring entity; and
• Municipal Engineer to put on record the date when projects completed are
accepted by the Municipality. Likewise, to carefully plan the contract duration of
infrastructure projects in the preparation of the program of works for a just and
fair labor cost.

We recommended that the BAC Secretariat be required to consolidate the purchase
requests received from various offices/departments and procure the same in
accordance with the intent and requirements of RA 9184 and its Implementing Rules
and Regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to closely
monitor and institute corrective measures on the findings of COA-TAS and
immediately inform the audit team of the courses of actions taken by the contractor.
Otherwise, demand payment from the contractor the amount of ₱491,563.93.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Office of the Human Resource
Management and the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to discontinue the practice of
allowing deductions of monthly loan amortizations from the personnel’s payroll other
than those mandated by law. Strictly comply with the GAA provision that monthly take
home pay of employee shall not be lower than Five Thousand Pesos (₱5,000.00).

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
disclose the required reconciliation either on the face of the SCBAA or in the Notes to
Financial Statements in compliance with paragraph 47 of PPSAS 24 to achieve
transparency in financial reporting.

observations.managementAction: The Municipality had exercised diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of the Municipality’s funds.
observations.reason: The municipality was now regularly monitoring the cash availability of the Municipality so as to avoid the incurrence of continuous cash overdraft. The total actual revenue collected during the year is more than the estimated revenue reported in the budget. Also, tax revenues increased during the year by ₱1,989,106.18 due to the efforts made by the Treasurer’s Office to collect real property taxes and corresponding penalties and due to tax amnesties granted by the Provincial Government. Trust funds received were utilized only for the specific purpose for which it was created. The Municipality had exercised proper control in the disposition of the funds. The municipality still incurred a cash insufficiency in the Trust Fund.

observations.recommendation:
• Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records to determine the cause of the discrepancies of their respective reports and prepare the necessary adjustments for a fair presentation of the financial statements. Ensure that the accounts of the agency shall be kept in such detail as necessary and observe consistency in keeping of records to safeguard against inaccurate or misleading information pursuant to Section 111 of PD 1445.
• Municipal Assessor to submit Monthly Report of Assessment of Real Properties and Individual Tax Payers to the Municipal Treasurer for reconciliation of their individual records. 
• Municipal Treasurer to maintain the subsidiary ledgers for the RPT and SET and reconcile with the Assessor’s records.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer had conducted reconciliation with the Assessor’s Office by comparing their ledgers with the Assessor’s reports and prepared the necessary adjustments for a fair presentation of the financial statements. They have noted the discrepancies (those ledger cards without tax declaration and property owners without ledger cards). The Municipal Treasurer added that the Assessor’s Office was still updating their master list. They emphasized that they are now preparing a system to settle the problem in the records of real property taxes.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant and Treasurer conducted reconciliation with the Assessor’s Office. The Municipal Treasurer validated that there were discrepancies and that some records were not 100% reconciled; there were ledger cards without tax declaration and records of the real property taxes were not completely reconciled. Limited manpower and the need for further reconciliation caused partial implementation.

observations.recommendation:
a. Coordinate again with the Provincial Accountant and reconcile their records on the provincial receivables/payables and reconcile the receivables and payables in the books of the Province to establish the correct balances of the intra-agency accounts.
b. Coordinate with the concerned officials and departments to obtain information on those long outstanding accounts. If found to have no valid claims against the Municipality, revert to the unappropriated surplus account as required under Section 98 of P.D. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The staff of the Municipal Accountant had conducted reconciliation with the Provincial Accountant. They verified with the Province and coordinated with the concerned officials and department heads to obtain information on those long outstanding accounts. However, there were still unreconciled balances.
observations.reason: Limited time was allotted to settle this issue due to other priority tasks. Some balances remained unreconciled and there were outstanding accounts yet to be settled.

observations.recommendation:
• The GSO Committee to conduct the physical count of all supplies semestrally and prepare the Report of Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI). Submit copies of RPCI to the Municipal Accountant and to the Audit Team within the prescribed period.
• Reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and establish the causes of discrepancies, whether items had already been issued, distributed and consumed to arrive at the correct balance in the Financial Statements.
• Maintain stock cards properly and supervise the receipt and issuance of materials and supplies.
• The Accounting Department to reconcile with the submitted RPCI of the GSO and ensure that purchases of supplies and materials, aside from those procured through petty cash be coursed through proper inventory account instead of directly charging them to expense.
• The Accounting Department to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to record the appropriate supplies expense accounts based on the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI).
observations.managementAction: The GSO planned to have a new system for the monitoring of the inventories and implementing this new system starting January of the year 2019. The Municipal Accountant and the GSO agreed to implement physical counts and to maintain required inventory records. The GSO was implementing a new system starting January 2019. The Accounting Office noted lack of manpower to fully maintain supplies ledger cards as prescribed. The GSO to prepare and maintain the Property Cards and update AREs for acquisitions; there were efforts to reconcile and update the records.
observations.reason: No RPCI was prepared by the GSO since they had not conducted the physical count of inventories. The Accounting Office has no enough manpower to prepare and maintain supplies ledger cards. Some purchases were not processed through inventory accounts due to practices and staffing constraints. The GSO planned to implement new systems starting January 2019.

observations.recommendation:
• The Inventory Committee headed by the Municipal GSO to prepare and maintain the inventory list by category and to prepare the RPCPPE and submit to the Municipal Accountant and to the Audit Team within the prescribed period.
• The Municipal GSO to update the Property Cards and ARE and ensure AREs are renewed at least every three years or whenever there is change in custodianship.
• The Municipal Accountant to prepare the lapsing schedule with complete information consistent with the balances presented in the financial statements.
• The Municipal Accountant to trace the records of all PPEs and provide the corresponding depreciation expenses.
• The Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain required PPE Ledger Cards to facilitate reconciliation with RPCPPE and accounting records.
• The Chairman of the Committee on Disposal to initiate/facilitate disposal of unserviceable properties in accordance with COA Circular No. 92-386 and make necessary adjusting entries.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee headed by the Municipal GSO had performed the actual and complete physical count of PPE and prepare inventory list by category. Property Cards for purchases of PPE were updated for the year 2017 and years below. AREs for current year acquisitions were updated. The Municipal Accountant prepared adjusting entry to correct the balance of the transportation equipment account. The Chairman of the Committee on Disposal submitted a request for disposal and the Municipal Accountant prepared adjusting entries. Some actions implemented but lacking full completion due to manpower constraints.
observations.reason: The Inventory Committee performed the physical count but reconciliation with accounting records have not been fully completed. The Accounting Office has no enough manpower to identify and trace all PPEs and to update all ledger cards. Some AREs and property cards were updated only for recent acquisitions and not for prior years. Disposal and adjusting entries were initiated but full implementation pending.

observations.recommendation:
• Municipal Accountant to submit the documents pertaining to prior years’ liabilities and payables to COA for verification and review.
• Municipal Accountant to review the validity of the Accounts Payable and Due to Officers and Employees accounts and to determine if there were unpaid valid claims or outstanding payables. If found to have no valid claims, request Sangguniang Bayan for reversion of the same to the unappropriated surplus.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant submitted documents pertaining to prior years’ payable which were available. The Municipal Accountant validated that there were no unpaid valid claims for these accounts payable. Some accounts payable which are outstanding were yet to be reverted; requests to the Sangguniang Bayan to revert the same were to be prepared.
observations.reason: Some supporting documents were no longer available for prior years’ payables and some payees could not be located. The reversion of certain long outstanding accounts require formal action by the Sangguniang Bayan and preparation of necessary documents which were pending.

observations.recommendation:
• Local School Board, Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the laws, rules and regulations and that SEF be utilized exclusively for the purpose for which it was created to promote the quality of education in every public schools and to enhance delivery of service to all constituents in the municipality.
• Municipal Treasurer to refrain from granting cash advances to accountable officers which payments can be made directly to the supplier/contractor in order for the Municipality to deduct and withhold applicable taxes and remit the same in accordance with the BIR Rules and Regulations.
• Stop the practice of allocating the five percent of the Special Education Fund budget for LDRRMF which is not in accordance with Sections 100(c) and 272 of RA 7160.
observations.managementAction: The management had stopped budgeting and paying expenses which are not priority expenditures under the Special Education Fund. Municipal Budget Officer had stopped allocating the five percent of the SEF budget for LDRRMF. The Local School Board and other implementing units prepared justifications and adjustments for disallowed items and submitted supporting documents where applicable.
observations.reason: Some activities previously charged to SEF were already adjusted and compliance improved; however, reiterations of similar findings occurred and some matters required further adjustments and documentation. Certain justifications and refunds were still being processed and some expenditures were charged to other office budgets.

observations.recommendation:
• Require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRM) to enforce the utilization of the 5% LDRRMF and ensure that programs and projects charged to the 5% LDRRMF are aligned with the intended appropriation.
• Require the Municipal Planning and Development Office and Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office to monitor utilization of the 5% LDRRMF and ensure that program and projects are aligned with intended appropriation.
• Require the Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate funds for the MOOE and Capital Outlay of the MDRRM Office to avoid charging non-priority expenditures to the calamity fund; and
• Require the MDRRM Office to ensure that future programs and projects charged to the 70% mitigation fund are implemented within the intended time to avoid delay.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Development Office and MDRRM had implemented projects charged to 70% Mitigation Fund. The Municipal Development Office had monitored utilization of 5% LDRRMF and ensured programs and projects were aligned with appropriation. Municipal Budget Officer monitored the utilization and ensured program and projects are aligned. The Municipality implemented substantial programs and projects under the 70% mitigation fund but there were delays noted in certain projects.
observations.reason: Municipality implemented many activities under the funds; however, some projects were delayed and some expenditures lacked proper documentation and alignment which required further adjustments and monitoring. There was a noted delay in implementation of a project charged to 70% mitigation fund amounting to ₱5,950,000.00.

observations.recommendation:
• The Municipal Planning and Development Officer and the Municipal Engineer to look into the implementation of projects under 20% Development Fund and ensure the feasibility of the projects and ensure that projects undertaken are prioritized and included in the Annual Investment Plan.
• Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to avoid charging expenditures that do not contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes and that the fund be utilized only for the projects identified and prioritized pursuant to the established rules and regulations.
• Ensure that all programs and projects charged to the 20% Development Fund are investment/capital expenditures in accordance with Joint Memorandum Circular.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Development Council and Municipal Planning and Development Office reviewed and ensured that projects charged to the 20% Development Fund constituted LGU priority programs. The Municipal Development Office ensured implementation and monitoring and reported on project status. Personnel in-charge reported implementation regularly and projects were executed.
observations.reason: For CY 2018, the 20% Development Fund was utilized for projects prioritized and identified, and the municipality attained substantial implementation (89% reported), but some unutilized balances remained and certain projects required further monitoring and completion.

observations.recommendation:
• Properly monitor and report regularly the status of implementation of the activities in the Gender and Development Plan to address the gender issues.
• Align the GAD Plans, Budget and its Annual Accomplishment Report for the program, projects and activities which are responsive to the gender issues and ensure planned PPAs are responsive, and processes and procedures are properly followed.
• Update the GAD Database to have an accurate basis that will strengthen the planning and budgeting processes for GAD and ensure that planned PPAs address gender issues and that objectives, performance indicators and targets are well-formulated.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality updated their GAD utilization and GAD Plan. The Municipality reported monitoring and implementation of various GAD activities. Some GAD activities were implemented and funded; however, other activities were not implemented due to reasons such as insufficient funds and lack of availability of DILG for certain processes.
observations.reason: Some GAD activities were conducted and funds utilized; however, some activities were not implemented due to insufficient funds, lack of necessary approvals, or unavailability of supporting agencies like DILG. The GAD Database updating depended on external data sources (PSA/CBMS) which were not fully available or current.

observations.recommendation:
• The Local Chief Executive, as Chairman of the LCPC, to instruct the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office and Municipal Planning and Development Officer to optimally utilize the allotted budget and implement projects/programs/activities enumerated in Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 and ensure that funds are used to achieve objectives of the law.
• The Municipal Planning and Development Officer and other offices to coordinate and implement activities to address children-related issues and ensure appropriate budgeting and programming.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Municipal Social Welfare and Development implemented activities charged to LCPC and some activities were implemented and charged to the Mayor’s Office. The Municipality implemented activities for the benefit of children charged to other budgets such as Mayor’s Office amounting to ₱70,240.00. The LCPC partners participated in planning and implementation.
observations.reason: Some activities were implemented but charged to other offices. The LCPC activities were not fully planned and implemented under LCPC budget, and comprehensive local development plans for children were not fully prepared; further coordination and resource allocation needed.

observations.recommendation:
• Require the Head of the Municipal Planning and Development Office to return to source agency the unexpended balance and coordinate with concerned heads to enforce utilization of the remaining amount of ₱1,629,176.64 and ₱1,000,000.00.
• Municipal Accountant to immediately submit liquidation to the source agency for refund of the unexpended balance and ensure future fund transfers are implemented within the timeframe to avoid delay in liquidation. Furnish COA Audit Team a copy of duly notarized and approved MOA.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal accountant submitted liquidation to the source agency and conducted liquidation for the unexpended balance of ₱800.00 and implemented projects funded from BUB in CY 2018. They took steps to remitted balances where applicable and ensured COA is furnished with documents.
observations.reason: The mentioned projects were implemented as of December 31, 2018 but the unexpended balance of ₱1,629,176.64 and unimplemented ₱1,000,000.00 required further action. One payee needed for preparation of check for refund was not given and other administrative steps pending. Some fund transfers were not fully remitted in time.

observations.recommendation:
• Municipal Planning and Development Officer to include among her priorities the construction of storeroom that can accommodate all documents generated by the municipality and its 42 barangays to be preserved for a period of ten (10) years as mandated by existing government auditing laws.
• Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate funds for expenses that will be incurred relative to the construction of storeroom. Ensure the project’s inclusion in the Annual Budget.
• Municipal Engineer to supervise the implementation and monitor accomplishments to ensure compliance with plans and specifications.
• Municipal Accountant to ensure that the project will be properly recorded in the books and its disbursements will be in accord with accounting rules.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal units noted the observation and planned for construction of storeroom; efforts and plans to allocate funds were considered. However, the space available was occupied by other units and no immediate construction implemented.
observations.reason: The matter was not prioritized by the concerned units. Funds were not yet appropriated and the project not yet implemented due to other priorities.

observations.recommendation:
• The Inventory Committee to conduct the actual physical count of all Property, Plant and Equipment at least once a year to ensure its existence, prepare Report of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) with complete information and submit to the Municipal Accountant and the Audit Team.
• The General Services Officer to prepare and maintain the Property Cards and update AREs for prior years’ acquisitions and ensure AREs renewed every three years or whenever change of custodianship.
• Municipal Accountant to prepare the lapsing schedule with complete information consistent with the balances presented in the financial statements, trace the records of all PPEs and provide corresponding depreciation expenses, and prepare and maintain PPE Ledger Cards.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee had performed the physical count and prepared inventory lists. The Property Cards for acquisitions were updated for certain years. The Municipal Accountant prepared lapsing schedule and traced some PPE records and prepared required ledger cards where manpower allowed.
observations.reason: The GSO performed inventory but reconciliation with accounting not fully completed. The Accounting Office lacked manpower to fully prepare and update all required PPE ledger cards and depreciation schedules. AREs and Property Cards for some prior years not fully updated.

observations.recommendation:
a. Local Finance Committee to adopt strategy to increase revenue/collections and monitor cash availability every month.
b. Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to exercise diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of Municipality’s funds and limit expenditures within total income realized.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality was now regularly monitoring the cash availability and had exercised diligence and proper control in disposition of funds.
observations.reason: The Municipality was regularly monitoring cash availability and actual revenue collected exceeded estimated revenue; however, there remained cash insufficiency in the Trust Fund.

observations.recommendation:
• Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records and determine the cause of discrepancies and prepare necessary adjustments for fair presentation.
• Municipal Assessor to coordinate and prepare necessary reconciliation documents and ensure consistency in records.
• Municipal Treasurer to exhaust efforts to collect delinquent real property taxes and encourage taxpayers to pay their dues on time and conduct verification of outstanding balances.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer and Accountant conducted reconciliations and coordination with the Assessor’s Office; Assessor still updating master list. Efforts made to collect taxes and reconcile records.
observations.reason: The Assessor’s and Treasurer’s offices were still updating records; some ledger cards lacked tax declarations and some taxpayers could not be located. Reconciliation ongoing and limited manpower.

observations.recommendation:
• The Inventory Committee to conduct physical counts semestrally and prepare RPCI and submit copies to Municipal Accountant and Audit Team.
• Reconcile count results with accounting records and establish causes of discrepancies. Maintain stock cards and Supplies Ledger Cards. Ensure purchases are recorded in inventory accounts and draw JEVs to record adjustments.
observations.managementAction: GSO planned to have a new system for monitoring inventories starting January 2019. Accounting Department to reconcile with submitted RPCI and prepare journal entries. Some supplies ledger cards not maintained due to lack of staff.
observations.reason: No RPCI prepared as GSO had not yet conducted physical count; Accounting Office lacked manpower to maintain supplies ledger cards; new system to be implemented starting January 2019.

observations.recommendation:
• The Municipal Accountant to reconcile the municipal records with the Province and request copies of JEVs from the Provincial Accountant showing all transactions recorded in the books of the Province.
• Reconcile reciprocal accounts to present correct balances of inter-agency receivables and payables in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Staff of the Municipal Accountant conducted reconciliation with the Province and believed that their subsidiary ledgers were sufficient; however, some unreconciled balances remained.
observations.reason: Limited time and coordination with the Province; some discrepancies remained unreconciled and the Province’s supporting documents were thought sufficient by the Municipality but further reconciliation required.

observations.recommendation:
• Municipal Development Council to ensure the Annual Investment Plan constitutes LGU priority programs and to ensure that annual budget integrates the 20% Development Fund and secure SB approval before implementation.
• Municipal Development Council to specify detailed descriptions, locations and cost of priority programs, projects and activities funded from 20% Development Fund and ensure such projects are capital/investment expenditures.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2018, the Municipal Development Council ensured that 20% Development Fund projects were prioritized and integrated into the annual budget. The Council ensured that projects were itemized and that expenditures were capital in nature where applicable.
observations.reason: The Municipality ensured itemized appropriations and complied with rules for CY 2018; they secured SB approvals and monitored project implementation.

observations.recommendation:
• Strictly observe laws, rules and regulations and ensure SEF is utilized exclusively for purposes for which it was created; avoid charging the SEF for programs/projects not allowed under DepEd Order No. 13 s. 2016.
• Stop the practice of allocating five percent of SEF budget for LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Budget Officer stopped allocating the five percent of SEF for LDRRMF. The Municipality ensured that SEF-funded projects complied with DepEd and DBM rules and adjusted disbursements accordingly.
observations.reason: The Municipality had stopped the practice and ensured SEF expenditures complied with rules for CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: • Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Office (MENRO) and Municipal Solid Waste Management Board to coordinate with Regional Office for approval of Environmental Management Plan 2018 and ensure 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan is approved and to require barangay officials to establish a fully operational MRF in every barangay or cluster of barangays as per Section 32 of RA 9003.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality exerted efforts to comply with RA 9003, conducted IEC activities, and provided financial assistance for barangays to establish MRFs; three barangays had operational MRFs and more were assisted.
observations.reason: MRF initiatives and IEC were undertaken; three barangays had operational MRFs and financing for additional MRFs provided; however, full barangay compliance was ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Local Finance Committee to adopt strategy in order to increase revenue/collections and monitor properly the availability of cash every month to ensure that all income due the Municipality be collected in full and assure continuous flow of funds and that the trust funds received be utilized only for the specific purpose for which it was created; and

b. Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer exercise diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of the Municipality’s funds and limit expenditures within the total income realized and collected during the year to avoid incurrence of cash overdraft.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant had prepared monthly reconciliations of cash and implemented controls. Management commented that since the estimated income were not fully realized by the Municipality and the PPAs were based on those estimates, projects which were not implemented and that are considered as a capital outlay were transferred in the continuing appropriation even though they were not sufficiently supported with available cash. Thus, after deduction of the liabilities and continuing appropriation, they continuously incur a cash overdraft. They also stated that in CY 2018 budget, they based their income on the income that could actually be realized and met through their efforts in enforcing collections.
observations.reason: Municipality failed to adopt effective strategies to increase revenue and fully monitor cash availability; projects were placed in continuing appropriations despite insufficient cash; prior year recommendations not fully acted upon.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to determine the cause of discrepancies of their respective reports and prepare the necessary adjustments for a fair presentation of the financial statements. Ensure that the accounts of the agency shall be kept in such detail as necessary and observe consistency in keeping of accounts to safeguard against inaccurate or misleading information pursuant to Section 111 of PD1445.
observations.managementAction: On the submitted letter of the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant, they had commented that they acknowledge the inaccuracy in the records as there was no reconciliation ever done between the Assessor’s Record of Assessed Values of Properties and the Real Property Tax Account Record of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office. Likewise, they agreed on the audit recommendation to reconcile their records and require the Municipal Assessor to submit Monthly Report of Assessment of Real Properties and Individual Tax Payers Assessment to the Municipal Treasurer.
observations.reason: No reconciliation done between Assessor, Treasurer and Accountant; subsidiary ledgers for RPT/SET not maintained; discrepancies increased compared to prior year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the municipal records with that of the Provincial Government of Batangas and settle the discrepancies between the balances of reciprocal accounts so that the Municipality will be able to present the correct balances of inter-agency receivables and payables in the financial statements. B. LapsesandInconsistencies intheUtilization amountingto ₱1,000,968.58.

 Charges against the Development Fund totaling ₱1,000,968.58 takes in the form
of Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses, not in accordance with section
2.3ofthesame JMC, taking into considerationthe costandentrepreneurship.
JMC No. 2017-01, dated February 22, 2017 was issued to updated guidelines on
the appropriation and utilization of no less than 20% of the IRA for development projects.
Section 3.0 of the said joint circular provides the allowable development projects
chargeable against the20% Development Fund, which are as follows:
“3.1Social Development
3.1.1 Construction or rehabilitation of health centers, rural health
unitsor hospitals,including purchase of lotfor thepurpose;
3.1.2Purchaseof ambulance andmedical equipment;
3.1.3 Construction or rehabilitation of local government-owned
potablewatersupply system;
3.1.4 Establishment or rehabilitation of Manpower Development
Centers;
3.1.5 Construction or rehabilitation of evacuation centers, including
purchaseof lot forthe purpose;
3.1.6 Construction of Special Drug Education Centers, and Drug
Treatment/Rehabilitation Centers, including purchase of lot for the
purpose;
3.1.7 Rehabilitation of historical sites classified as such by the
NationalHistoricalCommission of thePhilippines;
3.1.8 Purchase and development of land for the relocation of informal
settlers andrelocation ofvictims ofcalamities;
3.1.9 Construction or rehabilitation of multi-purpose halls, including
purchaseof tot forthe purpose;and
3.1.10Installationof street lighting system.
3.2Economic Development
3.2.1 Construction or rehabilitation of communal irrigation or water
impounding system; 3.2.2 Purchase or lease of post-harvest facilities,
such as farm or hand tractor with trailer, thresher and mechanical
driers;
3.2.3 Construction or rehabilitation of local roads or bridges,
including purchase of appropriate engineering equipment, such as
dump trucks, graders andpay loaders;
3.2.4 Capital expenditures related to the implementation of livelihood
or entrepreneurship/local economic development projects;
3.2.5 Development of alternative power or energy sources, such as,
butnot limited to, renewableenergy power plants;and
3.2.6 Amortization of loans used to finance development projects cited
in this JMC, subject to the 20% debt service cap prescribed under
Section 324(b) ofRA No.7160.
3.3Environmental Management
3.3.1Reforestation andurbangreening;
3.3.2 Construction or rehabilitation of sanitary landfills and materials
recovery facilities;
3.3.3 Purchase of garbage trucks and other equipment for
environmentalmanagement andprotection purposes;
3.3.4 Implementation of flood and erosion control projects, such as
rehabilitation and construction of drainage systems, de-silting of
rivers, andde-clogging ofcanals; and
3.3.5 Other environmental management projects that promote air and
water quality, as well as productivity of the coastal or freshwater
habitat, agricultural land and forest land, such as, but not limited to,
treatment of wastewater for conservation/re-use purposes, and
installationof air pollutioncontrol devices.”
In addition, Section 2.3 of the same JMC also provides provision on the nature of
the development projects to be taken up against the 20% Development Fund which are as
follows:
“All Development projects to be funded under the 20% DF shall
contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development
and environmental management outcomes of the LGU, and shall
partakethenatureof investment or capitalexpenditures.”

Post audit of disbursement vouchers as well as the Report on Utilization revealed
that ₱1,000,968.58 are in the form of repair and maintenance expenses of buildings, other
structures and motor vehicle as well as other maintenance and operating expenses.
Details were provided belowas follows:
Account Used Amount
Repairand Maintenance –Buildings and Other Structures ₱870,482.80
Repairand Maintenance –Machinery and Equipment 40,176.67
Repairand Maintenance –MotorVehicles 26,085.00
OtherMaintenance andOperating Expenses 64,224.11
TOTAL ₱1,000,968.58

It is worth mentioning though, that those projects and programs can contribute to
the restoration of those property, plant and equipment and aid as part of maintenance to
keep them in their good working condition. However, being maintenance and other
operating expenses in nature, already rendered them not in consonance with the provision
of the JMC that they should be capital expenditures or investment in nature, and should
have been charged to thenormal expenditures in thegeneral fund instead.

Based ontheforegoing observations, we recommend thefollowing:
Werecommended theMunicipalMayorto instructthefollowing:

 The Municipal Planning and Development Officer and the Municipal Engineer
to immediately look into projects listed in the 20% Development Fund and
ensure the feasibility of the identified priority projects. The plan should clearly
specify the detailed descriptions, locations and cost of priority programs,
projects andactivities fundedoutof20percentDevelopmentFund.
Likewise, require the personnel in-charge of implementing the project to report
regularly on the status of the projects for proper monitoring and evaluation of
14  theefficiency oftheimplementation.
 Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to avoid charging
expenditures that do not contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-
economic development and environmental management outcomes and that the
fund be utilized only for the projects identified and prioritized pursuant to the
establishedrules and regulations.
Likewise, ensure that programs, projects and activities charged to the fund
partakes investment and capital expenditures in accordance to the joint
memorandumcircular.

Management'sComment:
The Municipality had commented among others, the following reasons for the
non-utilization of the 20% Development Fund such as unexpected increase in population
and service requirements on barangays, various modifications requested by the intended
